audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 11, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for siege negotiation, eye-gouging and disability ethics, coercive covenant, Saul's cattle threat, troop figures, rescue battle, proposed execution of dissenters, clemency, and kingship renewal.

verses.restored: Nahash the Ammonite went up and encamped against Jabesh-gilead. All men of Jabesh said to Nahash, Cut a covenant with us, and we will serve you.
Nahash the Ammonite said to them, On this condition I will cut it with you: by gouging out every right eye and placing disgrace upon all Israel.
Elders of Jabesh said to him, Leave us seven days so we may send messengers through all territory of Israel. If there is no one to save us, we will come out to you.
Messengers came to Gibeah of Saul and spoke words in ears of people, and all people lifted their voice and wept.
Look, Saul came after cattle from field. Saul said, What is with people that they weep? They recounted to him words of men of Jabesh.
Spirit of Divine rushed upon Saul when he heard these words, and his anger burned greatly.

He took a pair of cattle, cut them in pieces, and sent them through all territory of Israel by hand of messengers, saying, Whoever does not come out after Saul and after Samuel, so shall it be done to his cattle. Dread of YHWH fell upon people, and they came out as one man.
He mustered them at Bezek: children of Israel were three hundred thousand and men of Judah thirty thousand.
They said to messengers who had come, Thus you shall say to men of Jabesh-gilead: Tomorrow salvation will be for you when sun is hot. Messengers came and told men of Jabesh, and they rejoiced.
Men of Jabesh said, Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you may do to us according to all that is good in your eyes.

On next day Saul put people in three companies. They came into middle of camp at morning watch and struck Ammon until heat of day. Those remaining were scattered, and no two of them were left together.
People said to Samuel, Who is the one saying, Shall Saul reign over us? Give the men, and we will put them to death.
Saul said, No man shall be put to death on this day, for today YHWH has made salvation in Israel.
Samuel said to people, Come, let us go to Gilgal and renew kingship there.
All people went to Gilgal, and there they made Saul king before YHWH in Gilgal. They sacrificed peace offerings before YHWH, and Saul and all men of Israel rejoiced greatly.

translation_notes: Nahash's demanded eye-gouging is torture, disability-making, political humiliation, and collective intimidation; blindness itself is never disgrace.
Jabesh's elders negotiate under siege. Their proposed submission is survival strategy, not cowardice.
Saul's anger is directed toward rescue, but his dismemberment of cattle and threat against nonparticipants are coercive mobilization.
Spirit language does not sanctify threats, conscription, animal killing, or all later actions of a leader.
Battle victory does not erase Ammonite humanity or authorize ethnic hatred against modern peoples.
The people seek to execute earlier dissenters. Saul's refusal is an important act of restraint and rejects retroactive punishment for criticism.
The renewal of kingship follows rescue but remains subject to chapter 8's warning about extraction and centralized power.
safety_note: Reject torture, eye-gouging, disability shame, coerced military mobilization, animal dismemberment as intimidation, ethnic hatred, and execution of political dissenters. Saul's rescue and clemency may be honored without turning war, monarchy, or Spirit-empowered anger into unquestionable models.
